name: PDEBench-v2
description: PDEBench is a benchmark for evaluating the end-to-end ability of large language models and AI agents to generate solver code for partial differential equations (PDEs). Given a natural-language description of a PDE problem, an agent must produce a complete finite-element solver (e.g. for FEniCSx/dolfinx, Firedrake, or deal.II). Version 2 (benchmark_v2.jsonl) contains 645 cases spanning 11 equation families (Poisson, heat, wave, Burgers, convection-diffusion, reaction-diffusion, Helmholtz, biharmonic, linear elasticity, Stokes, Navier-Stokes) and 7 mathematical types (elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic, nonlinear, reaction-diffusion, incompressible flow, mixed type). Each record specifies an oracle solver configuration, an evaluation strategy, the agent-tunable knobs, and the supported FEM libraries.
citeAs: @misc{pdebench2026, title={PDEBench: AI-Driven PDE Solver Code Generation Benchmark}, author={PDEBench Authors}, year={2026}}

distribution.name: benchmark_v2.jsonl
distribution.description: JSON-Lines file in which each line is a self-contained PDE benchmark case.

recordSet.name: cases
recordSet.description: One record per PDE benchmark case (645 cases in total).

field.name: equation_type
field.description: PDE family used to select the oracle solver. One of: poisson, heat, wave, burgers, convection_diffusion, reaction_diffusion, helmholtz, biharmonic, linear_elasticity, stokes, navier_stokes.

field.name: math_type
field.description: Mathematical classification tags (e.g., elliptic, parabolic, hyperbolic, nonlinear, reaction_diffusion, incompressible_flow, mixed_type). A case may have multiple tags.

field.name: oracle_config
field.description: Oracle solver configuration: PDE definition (coefficients, manufactured solution), domain, mesh, finite-element family/degree, boundary conditions, output format/grid, and linear-solver settings (ksp_type, pc_type, rtol). Stored as a JSON object.

field.name: evaluation_config
field.description: Evaluation strategy: target metric (e.g., rel_L2_grid), timeout in seconds, and tolerance multipliers for accuracy and time. Stored as a JSON object.

field.name: agent_knobs
field.description: List of solver knobs the agent is expected to choose (each with a name and natural-language description), e.g., mesh_resolution, element_degree, ksp_type, pc_type, rtol. Stored as a JSON array.

field.name: supported_libraries
field.description: FEM libraries that an agent may use to implement the solver (e.g., dolfinx, firedrake, dealii).

rai:dataLimitations: This dataset covers 11 PDE families — including nonlinear and coupled systems such as Navier–Stokes and reaction-diffusion equations — on canonical 2D and selected 3D domains, and may not generalise to singular or discontinuous solutions, highly irregular geometries, non-FEM numerical paradigms, or PDE regimes absent from the benchmark (e.g. high-Reynolds-number turbulence, fractional-order, or high-dimensional problems). It is not recommended for use as training data for systems subsequently evaluated on it, or as standalone evidence that agent-generated solver code is suitable for safety-critical engineering applications without independent validation.
rai:dataBiases: The benchmark is constructed from curated FEM textbook problems, introducing a selection bias toward well-posed, smooth, low-dimensional PDEs on regular domains, with elliptic/Poisson cases comprising roughly 53% of all instances. Nonlinear, hyperbolic, and coupled-flow regimes are under-represented, which may cause agents trained or selected on this benchmark to perform disproportionately well on elliptic problems while underperforming on more complex or irregular settings.
rai:personalSensitiveInformation: The dataset contains no personal or sensitive information in any of the following categories: Gender, Socio-economic status, Geography, Language, Age, Culture, Experience or Seniority, Health or medical data, Political or religious beliefs. Every record is a fully synthetic mathematical specification of a PDE problem and its solver configuration.
rai:dataUseCases: This dataset measures the ability of AI agents to translate natural-language PDE specifications into correct, runnable FEM solver code. Validated use cases: zero-shot and few-shot evaluation of LLMs and coding agents on FEM code generation (see accompanying paper). Not validated for agent fine-tuning and non-FEM numerical paradigms.
rai:dataSocialImpact: Positive impact: enables reproducible, standardised evaluation of AI agents on scientific code generation, supporting transparent progress tracking in computational science research. Risks: misuse as training data for systems subsequently evaluated on this benchmark, or over-interpreting benchmark scores as evidence that agent-generated solvers are safe for deployment in engineering practice. Mitigation: the dataset is released under CC-BY-4.0 with explicit usage guidance discouraging training use, and reference solutions are computed deterministically from numerical oracles, eliminating subjective human annotation bias.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: PDE Problem Template Design
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Research team manually designed problem templates covering 11 PDE families, specifying domain configurations, boundary conditions, coefficient ranges, and oracle solver parameters.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Synthetic Problem Instance Generation
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Problem instances were synthetically generated using Claude Opus 4.6 (Anthropic) and Gemini 3.1 Pro (Google) via structured prompting pipelines. Each model received a problem template and produced a natural-language PDE description along with a complete oracle solver configuration.

prov:wasGeneratedBy.prov:label: Oracle Execution and Quality Filtering
prov:wasGeneratedBy.sc:description: Every generated case was validated by executing the oracle FEM solver (FEniCSx/dolfinx, Firedrake, or deal.II) and verifying numerical convergence against the reference solution. Cases failing solver execution, convergence checks, or schema validation were discarded before the final dataset was assembled.
